question: Given the `filter` above on `startDate`, what does the `||/m` modifier match in the Operate API query?

options.text: Items whose `startDate` falls within the same minute as `2022-03-17 11:50:25`.
Items whose `startDate` falls within the same month as `2022-03-17`.
Items whose `startDate` is within `1` millisecond of `2022-03-17T11:50:25.729+0000`.
Items whose `startDate` exactly equals the provided ISO-8601 timestamp; the suffix is ignored.

a.text: Correct. The modifier table maps `||/m` to "Within a minute", so the filter matches all items whose `startDate` is in the same minute as the given timestamp.

b.text: `||/M` (uppercase M) means "Within a month". Lowercase `||/m` is "Within a minute", which is what the example uses.

c.text: There is no millisecond modifier; the finest-grained option in the table is `||/s` ("Within a second").

d.text: The modifier is significant; without it, the filter would require an exact-value match on the date. With `||/m`, Operate broadens the match to the minute window.

explanation: Date filters take the form `yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.SSSZZ` with an optional range modifier appended via `||/<unit>`. The example uses `||/m` (lowercase) which maps to "Within a minute", so any item whose `startDate` is in the same minute as `2022-03-17 11:50:25` matches.
